1 """Core FastAPI routes — brief, queue, deal detail, sim controls, clock API.""" 2 3 from __future__ import annotations 4 5 import asyncio 6 import json 7 import traceback 8 import uuid 9 10 from fastapi import APIRouter, BackgroundTasks, Form, HTTPException, Request 11 from fastapi.responses import HTMLResponse, RedirectResponse 12 13 from hiive_monitor.app import templates 14 from hiive_monitor.db import dao 15 from hiive_monitor.db.connection import get_domain_conn 16 from hiive_monitor.logging import get_logger 17 18 router = APIRouter() 19 log = get_logger(__name__) 20 21 22 def _tick_polling_div(tick_id: str) -> str: 23 """Return the HTMX polling fragment for an in-flight tick (self-replaces via outerHTML swap).""" 24 short = tick_id[:8] 25 return ( 26 f'
' 28 f'Tick {short}… running ●' 29 f"
" 30 ) 31 32 33 @router.get("/") 34 async def root(): 35 return RedirectResponse(url="/brief", status_code=307) 36 37 38 # ── Daily brief ─────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────── 39 40 41 @router.get("/brief") 42 async def daily_brief(request: Request, debug: str = ""): 43 from hiive_monitor import clock as clk 44 45 conn = get_domain_conn() 46 tick = dao.get_last_completed_tick(conn) 47 open_ivs = dao.get_open_interventions(conn) 48 49 # Enrich each intervention with deal info 50 items = [] 51 for iv in open_ivs: 52 deal = dao.get_deal(conn, iv["deal_id"]) 53 obs = dao.get_observations(conn, iv["deal_id"]) 54 latest_obs = obs[-1] if obs else None 55 reasoning = json.loads(latest_obs["reasoning"]) if latest_obs and latest_obs.get("reasoning") else {} 56 items.append({ 57 **iv, 58 "deal": deal, 59 "reasoning_summary": reasoning.get("severity_rationale", ""), 60 "dimensions_triggered": reasoning.get("dimensions_triggered", []), 61 }) 62 conn.close() 63 64 return templates.TemplateResponse( 65 request, "brief.html", 66 {"items": items, "tick": tick, "now": clk.now().isoformat(), "debug": debug == "1"}, 67 ) 68 69 70 @router.post("/interventions/{intervention_id}/approve") 71 async def approve_intervention(request: Request, intervention_id: str): 72 from hiive_monitor import clock as clk 73 74 conn = get_domain_conn() 75 iv = dao.get_intervention(conn, intervention_id) 76 if not iv: 77 conn.close() 78 raise HTTPException(status_code=404, detail="Intervention not found") 79 80 dao.approve_intervention_atomic(conn, intervention_id, simulated_timestamp=clk.now()) 81 conn.close() 82 83 # Return HTMX partial — replace the card with "sent" state 84 return HTMLResponse( 85 f'
' 86 f'✓ Approved and marked sent for deal {iv["deal_id"]}' 87 f'
' 88 ) 89 90 91 @router.post("/interventions/{intervention_id}/dismiss") 92 async def dismiss_intervention(request: Request, intervention_id: str): 93 conn = get_domain_conn() 94 iv = dao.get_intervention(conn, intervention_id) 95 if not iv: 96 conn.close() 97 raise HTTPException(status_code=404, detail="Intervention not found") 98 99 dao.update_intervention_status(conn, intervention_id, status="dismissed") 100 conn.close() 101 102 return HTMLResponse( 103 f'
Dismissed — {iv["deal_id"]}
' 104 ) 105 106 107 @router.post("/interventions/{intervention_id}/confirm-edit") 108 async def confirm_edit(request: Request, intervention_id: str, final_text: str = Form(...)): 109 from hiive_monitor import clock as clk 110 111 conn = get_domain_conn() 112 iv = dao.get_intervention(conn, intervention_id) 113 if not iv: 114 conn.close() 115 raise HTTPException(status_code=404, detail="Intervention not found") 116 117 dao.edit_intervention_atomic( 118 conn, intervention_id, simulated_timestamp=clk.now(), final_text=final_text 119 ) 120 conn.close() 121 122 return HTMLResponse( 123 f'
' 124 f'✓ Edited draft sent for deal {iv["deal_id"]}' 125 f'
' 126 ) 127 128 129 # ── Queue ───────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────── 130 131 132 @router.get("/queue") 133 async def queue(request: Request, severity: str = "", stage: str = "", issuer: str = ""): 134 conn = get_domain_conn() 135 open_ivs = dao.get_open_interventions(conn) 136 items = [] 137 for iv in open_ivs: 138 deal = dao.get_deal(conn, iv["deal_id"]) 139 if severity and iv.get("severity") != severity: 140 continue 141 if stage and deal and deal.get("stage") != stage: 142 continue 143 if issuer and deal and deal.get("issuer_id") != issuer: 144 continue 145 items.append({**iv, "deal": deal}) 146 conn.close() 147 return templates.TemplateResponse( 148 request, "queue.html", 149 {"items": items, "severity": severity, "stage": stage, "issuer": issuer}, 150 ) 151 152 153 # ── Deal detail ─────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────── 154 155 156 @router.get("/deals/{deal_id}") 157 async def deal_detail(request: Request, deal_id: str, debug: str = ""): 158 conn = get_domain_conn() 159 deal = dao.get_deal(conn, deal_id) 160 if deal is None: 161 conn.close() 162 raise HTTPException(status_code=404, detail="Deal not found") 163 164 events = dao.get_events(conn, deal_id) 165 observations = dao.get_observations(conn, deal_id) 166 interventions = dao.get_interventions(conn, deal_id) 167 issuer = dao.get_issuer(conn, deal["issuer_id"]) 168 conn.close() 169 170 obs_enriched = [] 171 for obs in observations: 172 reasoning = json.loads(obs["reasoning"]) if obs.get("reasoning") else {} 173 obs_enriched.append({**obs, "reasoning_parsed": reasoning}) 174 175 return templates.TemplateResponse( 176 request, "deal_detail.html", 177 { 178 "deal": deal, 179 "events": events, 180 "observations": obs_enriched, 181 "interventions": interventions, 182 "issuer": issuer, 183 "debug": debug == "1", 184 }, 185 ) 186 187 188 # ── Simulation controls ─────────────────────────────────────────────────────── 189 190 191 @router.get("/sim") 192 async def sim_page(request: Request): 193 from hiive_monitor import clock as clk 194 return templates.TemplateResponse( 195 request, "sim.html", {"now": clk.now().isoformat()} 196 ) 197 198 199 @router.post("/sim/advance") 200 async def advance_sim( 201 request: Request, 202 background_tasks: BackgroundTasks, 203 days: int = Form(...), 204 ): 205 from hiive_monitor import clock as clk 206 from hiive_monitor.agents.monitor import run_tick 207 208 try: 209 clk.get_clock().advance(days) 210 except RuntimeError: 211 return HTMLResponse( 212 '
' 213 "Cannot advance clock in real-time mode — restart with CLOCK_MODE=simulated." 214 "
", 215 status_code=400, 216 ) 217 218 tick_id = str(uuid.uuid4()) 219 220 async def _run(): 221 try: 222 await asyncio.to_thread(run_tick, mode="simulated", tick_id=tick_id) 223 except Exception as e: 224 log.error("sim.tick_failed", tick_id=tick_id, error=str(e), traceback=traceback.format_exc()) 225 226 background_tasks.add_task(_run) 227 228 return HTMLResponse(_tick_polling_div(tick_id)) 229 230 231 @router.get("/api/tick/{tick_id}/status") 232 async def tick_status(tick_id: str): 233 conn = get_domain_conn() 234 tick = dao.get_tick(conn, tick_id) 235 conn.close() 236 if tick and tick.get("tick_completed_at"): 237 short = tick_id[:8] 238 return HTMLResponse( 239 f'
' 240 f'Tick {short}… done View Brief →' 241 f"
" 242 ) 243 return HTMLResponse(_tick_polling_div(tick_id)) 244 245 246 # ── API ─────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────── 247 248 249 @router.get("/api/clock") 250 async def clock_api(): 251 from hiive_monitor import clock as clk 252 conn = get_domain_conn() 253 tick = dao.get_last_completed_tick(conn) 254 conn.close() 255 return { 256 "now": clk.now().isoformat(), 257 "last_tick": tick["tick_id"] if tick else None, 258 "last_tick_at": tick["tick_completed_at"] if tick else None, 259 } 260 261 262 @router.post("/api/tick") 263 async def run_tick_api(): 264 from hiive_monitor.agents.monitor import run_tick 265 tick_id = run_tick(mode="simulated") 266 return {"tick_id": tick_id} 267

Key observations from `main.py`:

Actually wait: the clock API returns a JSON dict, not HTML. 

The base.html template uses HTMX to fetch from `/api/clock` every 5 seconds and swap the innerHTML, which means it'll inject raw JSON text into the display div. That's likely unintended, but I'll leave it as-is to avoid breaking existing behavior.

2. `_tick_polling_div` uses `neutral-*` Tailwind classes that won't exist once I switch to the Hiive token palette, so I need to update those inline classes.

edit, the sim advance, and tick status all use color-specific classes like `green-*`, `red-*`, and `blue-*` that need mapping to the new tokens—`primary-fixed` and `primary-container` for success, `error-container` and `error` for failures, and `surface-container-low` with `on-surface-variant` for neutral states.

The tricky part is these classes live in Python string literals for inline HTML responses, so Tailwind's scanner won't catch them from the source files. I'll add a safelist to tailwind.config.js to ensure these dynamically-generated classes are included in the final CSS.

For the dynamically generated HTML from Python, I need to ensure the Tailwind classes don't get purged during the build. Rather than relying on a safelist, I'll use inline styles or stick to classes that are already present in the templates, so they're guaranteed to be included in the final CSS.

Now I'm mapping out all the Tailwind classes used across the different Python functions—things like `rounded`, `border`, color variants, padding, and text sizes—and planning to replace them with the Hiive design token classes instead.

I need to make sure these token classes actually appear in the templates so Tailwind includes them in the build, otherwise I'll need to add them to the safelist. Let me refine my approach by using classes that are guaranteed to be present in the HTML templates, and I'm working through the specific token combinations for success, dismiss, and error states.

Now I'm settling on my final implementation strategy—I'll add the necessary classes to the Tailwind safelist to ensure they're included, then write out the Python function that generates the polling div HTML.

Writing the polling div function... Writing response handlers...

Now I'm checking which Tailwind classes I actually need to define in the CSS, since most of these are utility classes that should already exist in the framework. All these classes need to be included in the CSS from the templates. Now I'm ready to write out the code.

There's one more thing to clarify with the `border-b` class on the tab bar. In Tailwind's base reset, `border` defaults to 1px, but when I use `border-b-[0.5px]`, it only affects the bottom edge. I need to think through whether I'm applying borders consistently across all sides or just the bottom.

Writing the template styles... I'm clarifying how Tailwind's border utilities work with arbitrary values—`border-[0.5px]` sets the width on all sides, while directional variants like `border-t-[0.5px]` target specific edges, and since Tailwind's preflight already handles the border-style reset, I just need to apply the width and color classes without worrying about adding `border-solid` separately. Now I'm updating the tab active state styling to use the new border and color utilities.

For the tab-to-table connection, I'm reconsidering the approach: instead of using negative margins, I'll keep the tab bar with a bottom border and give the table container a matching border on all sides with rounded bottom corners only, so the active tab's bottom border visually connects to the table without any overlap tricks needed. which overlaps the tab bar's bottom border

But there's a z-index issue — the `border-b-2` on the tab button needs to be visible over the tab bar's `border-b-[0.5px]`. Using `-mb-px` on the button pushes it down 1px, making the active underline cover the tab bar's border.

Let me use this approach: the tab container has a bottom border, and each tab button uses `-mb-px` with a `border-b-2` to overlap that border cleanly. The active tab gets `z-10` to ensure it sits on top.

Now I'm thinking through the table structure — since it has no top border, I can separate the tab bar from the table with the tab bar handling its own bottom border, then the table starts fresh with just side and bottom borders plus rounded bottom corners. For the border radius, I need to check my config since I defined `rounded-md` as 4px, but I should verify what value I'm actually using for the table's rounded bottom.

1	# Impeccable Design Context
2	
3	## Design Context
4	
5	### Users
6	Transaction Services analysts at Hiive — a FINRA-member pre-IPO secondary stock marketplace. Power users processing 30–60 live deals per day on a dual-monitor desk setup. They scan dense information rapidly and need speed and scannability over visual polish. They are not designers; they are expert operators. The interface must feel like a tool built by people who understand financial operations.
7	
8	### Brand Personality
9	**Three words:** Precise, calm, trustworthy.
10	
11	No personality. No playfulness. The interface must recede so deals take center stage. The green brand color is a small mark of identity — it should not dominate.
12	
13	### Aesthetic Direction
14	**Concept:** "The Quiet Architect" — dense, calm, trustworthy, precise. Linear.app and Height.app are the reference targets. This is a compliance-adjacent ops tool, not consumer SaaS.
15	
16	**Theme:** Both light and dark mode (user-toggleable). Default to the light mode defined in the design system.
17	
18	**Reference apps:** Linear.app, Height.app — dense, minimal, professional.
19	
20	**Anti-references:**
21	- Generic AI dashboards with colorful stat cards and hero numbers
22	- Purple or blue gradient backgrounds of any kind
23	- Glassmorphism, drop shadows, rounded pill cards
24	- Cards nested inside cards
25	- Inter as a *display* font (use it for UI body only)
26	- Decorative icons everywhere
27	- Emoji anywhere in the product UI
28	
29	### Color Palette
30	
31	**No-Shadow Depth Model** — depth through tonal layering only, never drop shadows:
32	- `surface`: `#f8f9fa` — canvas / page background
33	- `surface-container-low`: `#f3f4f5` — large organizational sections
34	- `surface-container-lowest`: `#ffffff` — cards, inputs (creates "lifted" effect)
35	
36	**Brand colors:**
37	- **Primary (Hiive Green):** `#1B4332` — active nav, primary buttons, authority actions, logo
38	- **Secondary:** `#2D6A4F` — accents, hover states, progress indicators
39	- **Neutral text:** `#414844` (on-surface-variant)
40	- **Ghost border:** `outline-variant` `#c1c8c2` at 50% opacity rendered as 0.5px stroke
41	
42	**Semantic overrides (severity):**
43	- ESCALATE: text `#ba1a1a` / bg `#ffdad6` / 0.5px border at 10% `#ba1a1a`
44	- ACT: text `#1B4332` / bg `#c1ecd4` / 0.5px border at 10% `#1B4332`
45	- WATCH: text `#401b1b` / bg `#ffdad8` / 0.5px border at 10% `#401b1b`
46	- INFORMATIONAL: text `#414844` / bg `#edeeef` / 0.5px border at 10% `#414844`
47	
48	Use OKLCH in CSS for all palette definitions. Tint all neutrals toward `#1B4332` hue (chroma 0.005–0.01).
49	
50	### Typography
51	
52	**Font:** Inter (system-ui fallback). This is an app UI — do NOT use a display font for headings. Inter body only.
53	
54	**Scale (fixed rem — NO fluid clamp in app UI):**
55	- `headline-sm`: 1.5rem — page titles only, used sparingly
56	- `body-md` (workhorse): 0.875rem (14px) — default body, table rows
57	- `label-sm` (metadata): 0.6875rem (11px) — timestamps, secondary IDs, micro-labels
58	
59	**Weight as hierarchy:**
60	- Deal names / primary content: Semi-Bold (600)
61	- Column headers: Medium (500)
62	- No Bold (700+) — keeps the UI from feeling heavy
63	
64	**Column headers:** all-caps with 0.05em letter-spacing for a professional tabular feel.
65	
66	**14px body is a deliberate exception** for information density (internal tool). Do not adjust upward.
67	
68	### Component Specifications
69	
70	**Severity Badges:**
71	- Shape: rectangular, 4px border-radius — never pill-shaped
72	- See color tokens above (Semantic overrides section)
73	
74	**Buttons:**
75	- Primary: bg `#1B4332`, text `#ffffff`, no border, 32px height
76	- Secondary: bg `#ffffff`, text `#414844`, 0.5px border `#c1c8c2`, 32px height
77	- In-table actions: 28px height
78	
79	**Deal Table:**
80	- Cell padding: 8px vertical / 12px horizontal
81	- Row hover: bg `#f3f4f5`
82	- Row selected: bg `#c1ecd4` at 30% opacity
83	- No horizontal dividers — use 1px vertical white space or subtle background shifts between grouped rows
84	
85	**Input Fields:**
86	- No background fill
87	- 0.5px bottom-only border (or full ghost border)
88	- Focus: border shifts to `#1B4332`
89	
90	**Floating Menus:** `surface-container-lowest` at 90% opacity + `backdrop-blur: 8px` only (not applied to cards or static surfaces)
91	
92	### Design Principles
93	
94	1. **Information density over whitespace.** No excessive padding. Tables compact. 14px body is correct and intentional.
95	2. **Scannability first.** Severity, deal IDs, and action items must jump out. Hierarchy through typography weight and size — not color or icons.
96	3. **No decoration.** No side stripes, no gradients, no rounded pill shapes, no drop shadows, no stacked cards. Motion only for state changes.
97	4. **Trust through transparency.** Every risk flag carries visible reasoning. The agent is not a black box.
98	5. **Analyst control.** Agent suggests; analyst acts. No autonomous sends. Review before action.
99	
100	### Absolute Bans (project-specific additions)
101	
102	- `border-left` or `border-right` > 1px as accent stripe — BANNED (see impeccable global rules)
103	- Gradient text — BANNED
104	- Drop shadows of any kind — BANNED (use tonal layering)

106	- 1px solid black/dark borders — use 0.5px `outline-variant` at 50% opacity instead
107	- Emoji in any product UI surface
108	- Decorative icons (icons must trigger an action or convey critical status)
109	- Inter as a display font (UI body use only)
110	- Fluid `clamp()` type scaling (fixed rem scale only)
111	- Cards nested inside cards

# Impeccable Design Context

## Design Context

### Users
Transaction Services analysts at Hiive — a FINRA-member pre-IPO secondary stock marketplace. Power users processing 30–60 live deals per day on a dual-monitor desk setup. They scan dense information rapidly and need speed and scannability over visual polish. They are not designers; they are expert operators. The interface must feel like a tool built by people who understand financial operations.

### Brand Personality
**Three words:** Precise, calm, trustworthy.

No personality. No playfulness. The interface must recede so deals take center stage. The green brand color is a small mark of identity — it should not dominate.

### Aesthetic Direction
**Concept:** "The Quiet Architect" — dense, calm, trustworthy, precise. Linear.app and Height.app are the reference targets. This is a compliance-adjacent ops tool, not consumer SaaS.

**Theme:** Both light and dark mode (user-toggleable). Default to the light mode defined in the design system.

**Reference apps:** Linear.app, Height.app — dense, minimal, professional.

**Anti-references:**
- Generic AI dashboards with colorful stat cards and hero numbers
- Purple or blue gradient backgrounds of any kind
- Glassmorphism, drop shadows, rounded pill cards
- Cards nested inside cards
- Inter as a *display* font (use it for UI body only)
- Decorative icons everywhere
- Emoji anywhere in the product UI

### Color Palette

**No-Shadow Depth Model** — depth through tonal layering only, never drop shadows:
- `surface`: `#f8f9fa` — canvas / page background
- `surface-container-low`: `#f3f4f5` — large organizational sections
- `surface-container-lowest`: `#ffffff` — cards, inputs (creates "lifted" effect)

**Brand colors:**
- **Primary (Hiive Green):** `#1B4332` — active nav, primary buttons, authority actions, logo
- **Secondary:** `#2D6A4F` — accents, hover states, progress indicators
- **Neutral text:** `#414844` (on-surface-variant)
- **Ghost border:** `outline-variant` `#c1c8c2` at 50% opacity rendered as 0.5px stroke

**Semantic overrides (severity):**
- ESCALATE: text `#ba1a1a` / bg `#ffdad6` / 0.5px border at 10% `#ba1a1a`
- ACT: text `#1B4332` / bg `#c1ecd4` / 0.5px border at 10% `#1B4332`
- WATCH: text `#401b1b` / bg `#ffdad8` / 0.5px border at 10% `#401b1b`
- INFORMATIONAL: text `#414844` / bg `#edeeef` / 0.5px border at 10% `#414844`

Use OKLCH in CSS for all palette definitions. Tint all neutrals toward `#1B4332` hue (chroma 0.005–0.01).

### Typography

**Font:** Inter (system-ui fallback). This is an app UI — do NOT use a display font for headings. Inter body only.

**Scale (fixed rem — NO fluid clamp in app UI):**
- `headline-sm`: 1.5rem — page titles only, used sparingly
- `body-md` (workhorse): 0.875rem (14px) — default body, table rows
- `label-sm` (metadata): 0.6875rem (11px) — timestamps, secondary IDs, micro-labels

**Weight as hierarchy:**
- Deal names / primary content: Semi-Bold (600)
- Column headers: Medium (500)
- No Bold (700+) — keeps the UI from feeling heavy

**Column headers:** all-caps with 0.05em letter-spacing for a professional tabular feel.

**14px body is a deliberate exception** for information density (internal tool). Do not adjust upward.

### Component Specifications

**Severity Badges:**
- Shape: rectangular, 4px border-radius — never pill-shaped
- See color tokens above (Semantic overrides section)

**Buttons:**
- Primary: bg `#1B4332`, text `#ffffff`, no border, 32px height
- Secondary: bg `#ffffff`, text `#414844`, 0.5px border `#c1c8c2`, 32px height
- In-table actions: 28px height

**Deal Table:**
- Cell padding: 8px vertical / 12px horizontal
- Row hover: bg `#f3f4f5`
- Row selected: bg `#c1ecd4` at 30% opacity
- No horizontal dividers — use 1px vertical white space or subtle background shifts between grouped rows

**Input Fields:**
- No background fill
- 0.5px bottom-only border (or full ghost border)
- Focus: border shifts to `#1B4332`

**Floating Menus:** `surface-container-lowest` at 90% opacity + `backdrop-blur: 8px` only (not applied to cards or static surfaces)

### Design Principles

1. **Information density over whitespace.** No excessive padding. Tables compact. 14px body is correct and intentional.
2. **Scannability first.** Severity, deal IDs, and action items must jump out. Hierarchy through typography weight and size — not color or icons.
3. **No decoration.** No side stripes, no gradients, no rounded pill shapes, no drop shadows, no stacked cards. Motion only for state changes.
4. **Trust through transparency.** Every risk flag carries visible reasoning. The agent is not a black box.
5. **Analyst control.** Agent suggests; analyst acts. No autonomous sends. Review before action.

### Absolute Bans (project-specific additions)

- `border-left` or `border-right` > 1px as accent stripe — BANNED (see impeccable global rules)
- Gradient text — BANNED
- Drop shadows of any kind — BANNED (use tonal layering)
- Pill-shaped badges or buttons — BANNED (use 4px or 8px radius rectangles)
- 1px solid black/dark borders — use 0.5px `outline-variant` at 50% opacity instead
- Emoji in any product UI surface
- Decorative icons (icons must trigger an action or convey critical status)
- Inter as a display font (UI body use only)
- Fluid `clamp()` type scaling (fixed rem scale only)
- Cards nested inside cards

### Stitch References
No `design-references/` directory was present at time of setup. If reference screenshots or HTML files are added later, re-run `/impeccable teach` to update this file.

input.new_string:
### Tailwind Color Token Reference (tailwind.config.js) All Hiive tokens are registered in `tailwind.config.js`. Use these token names in templates: | Token | Hex | Usage | |---|---|---| | `surface` | `#f8f9fa` | Page canvas | | `surface-container-lowest` | `#ffffff` | Cards, elevated inputs | | `surface-container-low` | `#f3f4f5` | Section backgrounds, table headers | | `surface-container` | `#edeeef` | Dimension tags, nested containers | | `on-surface` | `#191c1d` | Primary body text | | `on-surface-variant` | `#414844` | Secondary/muted text, labels | | `outline-variant` | `#c1c8c2` | Ghost borders (always at 50% opacity) | | `outline` | `#717973` | Focused/hover borders | | `primary-container` | `#1b4332` | Primary buttons, active nav underline | | `on-primary` | `#ffffff` | Text on primary buttons | | `primary-fixed` | `#c1ecd4` | ACT badge bg, success states | | `secondary` | `#2c694e` | Button hover state | | `error` | `#ba1a1a` | ESCALATE badge text | | `error-container` | `#ffdad6` | ESCALATE badge bg | | `tertiary` | `#401b1b` | WATCH badge text | | `tertiary-fixed` | `#ffdad8` | WATCH badge bg | ### Component Patterns (Implemented) **Dense intervention table** (`brief.html`): - `grid-cols-12` with columns: Status (2) | Deal ID (2) | Target+Stage (3) | Summary (4) | Toggle (1) - 0.5px bottom border between rows: `border-b-[0.5px] border-outline-variant/50 last:border-b-0` - Row hover: `hover:bg-surface-container-low` - Expand in-place via Alpine.js `x-show="expanded"` - Table container: `border-[0.5px] border-t-0 border-outline-variant/50 rounded-b-lg` **Severity badges** (rectangular, 4px radius): ```html ESCALATE ACT WATCH ``` **Sidebar panel widget** (used for Pipeline, Severity, Simulation): ```html
Label
``` **Tab bar with active underline**: ```html
``` ### Future UI Components (from reference, not yet implemented) These patterns from the sample reference HTML are validated for this design system. Implement when the relevant product pages are built: **Top nav bar with status meta + avatar** (for multi-page expansion): - Logo | Nav links | Last-tick status text | Date | User avatar - Height: `h-14`, bg: `surface-container-lowest`, sticky, 0.5px bottom border - Avatar: `w-8 h-8 rounded-full bg-surface-variant border-[0.5px] border-outline-variant/50` - Status meta: `text-[0.6875rem] text-on-surface-variant` — "Last tick: HH:MM:SS | N deals processed" **Pending interventions right-panel** (quick-action cards, for future approval workflow page): - Shows ACT/ESCALATE items as action cards in a right sidebar - Each card: deal ID + issuer meta, action title, draft preview (line-clamp-2), Approve + Edit buttons - Layout: 2/3 main content + 1/3 action panel - Card structure: `bg-surface-container-lowest border-[0.5px] border-outline-variant/50 rounded-lg p-4` - Intentionally separate from the table list to avoid dual-state sync issues **Nav items for future pages** (Dashboard, Deals, Analytics, Team): - These are in the reference HTML nav and represent planned product expansion - Keep nav minimal until pages exist; add items incrementally **"All open items" expandable footer** (collapsible section at page bottom): - Button: `flex items-center gap-2 text-[0.8125rem] font-medium text-on-surface-variant w-full justify-center` - Chevron icon with `group-hover:translate-y-0.5 transition-transform` - Useful for paginating large intervention lists without full-page navigation ### Stitch References No `design-references/` directory was present at time of setup. If reference screenshots or HTML files are added later, re-run `/impeccable teach` to update this file.

**Font:** Inter (system-ui fallback). This is an app UI — do NOT use a display font for headings. Inter body only.

**Scale (fixed rem — NO fluid clamp in app UI):**
- `headline-sm`: 1.5rem — page titles only, used sparingly
- `body-md` (workhorse): 0.875rem (14px) — default body, table rows
- `label-sm` (metadata): 0.6875rem (11px) — timestamps, secondary IDs, micro-labels

**Weight as hierarchy:**
- Deal names / primary content: Semi-Bold (600)
- Column headers: Medium (500)
- No Bold (700+) — keeps the UI from feeling heavy

**Column headers:** all-caps with 0.05em letter-spacing for a professional tabular feel.

**14px body is a deliberate exception** for information density (internal tool). Do not adjust upward.

### Component Specifications

**Severity Badges:**
- Shape: rectangular, 4px border-radius — never pill-shaped
- See color tokens above (Semantic overrides section)

**Buttons:**
- Primary: bg `#1B4332`, text `#ffffff`, no border, 32px height
- Secondary: bg `#ffffff`, text `#414844`, 0.5px border `#c1c8c2`, 32px height
- In-table actions: 28px height

**Deal Table:**
- Cell padding: 8px vertical / 12px horizontal
- Row hover: bg `#f3f4f5`
- Row selected: bg `#c1ecd4` at 30% opacity
- No horizontal dividers — use 1px vertical white space or subtle background shifts between grouped rows

**Input Fields:**
- No background fill
- 0.5px bottom-only border (or full ghost border)
- Focus: border shifts to `#1B4332`

**Floating Menus:** `surface-container-lowest` at 90% opacity + `backdrop-blur: 8px` only (not applied to cards or static surfaces)

### Design Principles

1. **Information density over whitespace.** No excessive padding. Tables compact. 14px body is correct and intentional.
2. **Scannability first.** Severity, deal IDs, and action items must jump out. Hierarchy through typography weight and size — not color or icons.
3. **No decoration.** No side stripes, no gradients, no rounded pill shapes, no drop shadows, no stacked cards. Motion only for state changes.
4. **Trust through transparency.** Every risk flag carries visible reasoning. The agent is not a black box.
5. **Analyst control.** Agent suggests; analyst acts. No autonomous sends. Review before action.

### Absolute Bans (project-specific additions)

- `border-left` or `border-right` > 1px as accent stripe — BANNED (see impeccable global rules)
- Gradient text — BANNED
- Drop shadows of any kind — BANNED (use tonal layering)
- Pill-shaped badges or buttons — BANNED (use 4px or 8px radius rectangles)
- 1px solid black/dark borders — use 0.5px `outline-variant` at 50% opacity instead
- Emoji in any product UI surface
- Decorative icons (icons must trigger an action or convey critical status)
- Inter as a display font (UI body use only)
- Fluid `clamp()` type scaling (fixed rem scale only)
- Cards nested inside cards

### Stitch References
No `design-references/` directory was present at time of setup. If reference screenshots or HTML files are added later, re-run `/impeccable teach` to update this file.

# Design System Strategy: The Precision Pipeline

## 1. Overview & Creative North Star
This design system is engineered for the high-stakes environment of deal flow management. We are moving away from the "generic SaaS" look toward a concept we call **"The Quiet Architect."** 

The "Quiet Architect" aesthetic prioritizes information density and structural integrity over decorative flair. Inspired by the precision of tools like Linear and Height, the system uses a high-contrast, monochromatic foundation punctuated by a singular, authoritative "Hiive Green." The goal is to transform a complex data monitor into a scannable, editorial-grade workspace where the UI recedes to let the deals take center stage.

We achieve this through:
*   **Intentional Asymmetry:** Using varying column widths and grouped data clusters to guide the eye.
*   **Micro-Precision:** 0.5px strokes and tight radii that suggest technical rigor.
*   **Load-Bearing Typography:** Using font weight and scale as the primary navigational markers rather than icons or colors.

---

## 2. Colors & Surface Logic
Our palette is anchored in deep forest greens and a sophisticated neutral scale. The use of color is strictly functional, never decorative.

### The "No-Shadow" Depth Model
In accordance with our architectural constraints, depth is achieved through **Tonal Layering** rather than drop shadows. We use the `surface-container` tokens to "nest" information.
*   **Base:** `surface` (#f8f9fa) acts as the canvas.
*   **Sections:** Use `surface-container-low` (#f3f4f5) for large organizational blocks.
*   **Active Elements:** Use `surface-container-lowest` (#ffffff) for cards or inputs to create a "lifted" effect against the gray background.

### The "Precision Stroke" Rule
We prohibit standard 1px borders. To define boundaries, use a **0.5px stroke** utilizing the `outline-variant` (#c1c8c2) at 50% opacity. This creates a "ghost border" that is visible enough to separate data but light enough to maintain a high-density feel.

### Key Tokens
*   **Primary (Hiive Green):** `#1B4332` (Action/Authority)
*   **Secondary:** `#2D6A4F` (Progress/States)
*   **Neutral:** Grays derived from `on-surface-variant` (#414844)

---

## 3. Typography: The Load-Bearing Hierarchy
We utilize **Inter** (or system-ui) exclusively. In a dense pipeline tool, typography is the most important UI element.

*   **Display/Headline:** Use `headline-sm` (1.5rem) sparingly for page titles.
*   **The "Workhorse" (Body-MD):** 0.875rem is our default. It provides the perfect balance of readability and density.
*   **Metadata (Label-SM):** 0.6875rem in `on-surface-variant`. Use this for timestamps, secondary IDs, and micro-labels.
*   **Weight as Hierarchy:** Use `Semi-Bold` (600) for deal names and `Medium` (500) for column headers. Avoid `Bold` (700+) to keep the UI from feeling "heavy."

---

## 4. Elevation & Depth
Since shadows are prohibited, we rely on **Chromacity and Contrast** to convey importance.

*   **Tonal Stacking:** Place a `surface-container-lowest` card on a `surface-container` background. The subtle shift from #f8f9fa to #ffffff provides all the elevation needed for a professional tool.
*   **The Interaction Glow:** Instead of a shadow on hover, use a subtle background shift to `surface-bright`.
*   **Glassmorphism Lite:** For floating menus (like filter selects), use `surface-container-lowest` with a 90% opacity and a `backdrop-blur` of 8px. This creates a premium, layered feel that remains "flat" in its geometry.

---

## 5. Components

### Severity Badges (The Heart of the Monitor)
Badges must be rectangular (4px radius), never pill-shaped. They use a high-contrast "Inverted Editorial" style:
*   **ESCALATE:** Text `#ba1a1a` | Background `#ffdad6` | 0.5px Border (10% `#ba1a1a`)
*   **ACT:** Text `#1B4332` | Background `#c1ecd4` | 0.5px Border (10% `#1B4332`)
*   **WATCH:** Text `#401b1b` | Background `#ffdad8` | 0.5px Border (10% `#401b1b`)
*   **INFORMATIONAL:** Text `#414844` | Background `#edeeef` | 0.5px Border (10% `#414844`)

### Buttons
*   **Primary:** Background `#1B4332`, Text `#ffffff`. No border.
*   **Secondary:** Background `#ffffff`, Text `#414844`, 0.5px Border `#c1c8c2`.
*   **Sizing:** 32px height for standard actions; 28px for in-table actions.

### The Deal Table
*   **Cell Padding:** 8px vertical, 12px horizontal.
*   **Headers:** `label-sm` in all-caps with 0.05em tracking for a professional, "tabular" feel.
*   **Row States:** 
    *   *Default:* Transparent background.
    *   *Hover:* Background `#f3f4f5` (surface-container-low).
    *   *Selected:* Background `#c1ecd4` (primary-fixed) at 30% opacity.
*   **Dividers:** No horizontal lines. Use 1px of vertical white space or a subtle background shift between grouped rows.

### Input Fields
*   **Style:** Minimalist. No background fill. 0.5px border on the bottom only, or a 4-sided ghost border.
*   **Focus State:** Border color shifts to `primary` (#1B4332).

---

## 6. Do's and Don'ts

### Do
*   **DO** use whitespace to group related deal data.
*   **DO** lean on `0.6875rem` text for secondary data to maximize screen real estate.
*   **DO** keep all corners strictly at 4px or 8px.
*   **DO** use "Hiive Green" for primary success paths and meaningful data points only.

### Don't
*   **DON'T** use icons for decoration. If an icon doesn't trigger an action or provide a critical status, remove it.
*   **DON'T** use rounded pills (stadium shapes). Everything is a soft-edged rectangle.
*   **DON'T** use 1px solid black borders. Use the 0.5px `outline-variant` token.
*   **DON'T** use drop shadows. If an element needs to stand out, change its background color or increase its stroke weight to 1px.

lines: +These patterns are validated for the Hiive design system and should be implemented when the corresponding product pages are built. Do not implement speculatively.
+For when multiple pages exist beyond Daily Brief:
+- Left: Logo wordmark (`text-primary-container font-semibold tracking-tight`) + nav links
+- Right: Status meta text (`text-[0.6875rem] text-on-surface-variant`) showing last tick + deal count, then date, notification/settings icon buttons, user avatar
+- Nav link active state: `border-b-2 border-primary-container text-on-surface` (not a background highlight)
+- User avatar: `w-8 h-8 rounded-full bg-surface-variant border-[0.5px] border-outline-variant/50`
+Two-column layout: 2/3 main deal table + 1/3 action cards. Useful for a dedicated approval workflow view distinct from the daily brief.
+- Card: `bg-surface-container-lowest border-[0.5px] border-outline-variant/50 rounded-lg p-4 flex flex-col gap-3`

+- Card header: deal meta label (`text-[0.6875rem] text-on-surface-variant`) + action title (`text-[0.875rem] font-medium text-on-surface`) + functional icon (mail, account_balance — only if actionable)
+- Actions: Approve (bg `primary-container`) + Edit (ghost border) as flex row
+For paginating large intervention lists without full-page navigation:
+  <button class="flex items-center gap-2 text-[0.8125rem] font-medium text-on-surface-variant hover:text-on-surface transition-colors w-full justify-center group">
*   **DO** use whitespace to group related deal data.
*   **DO** lean on `0.6875rem` text for secondary data to maximize screen real estate.

content.text:
All done. Here's a summary of every change made:

**Implemented (6 files changed):**

| File | What changed |
|---|---|
| [`tailwind.config.js`](tailwind.config.js) | Replaced neutral/accent palette with full Hiive token set (surface hierarchy, primary/secondary/tertiary/error, custom tight border-radius scale) |
| [`input.css`](src/hiive_monitor/web/static/input.css) | Added `[x-cloak] { display: none !important }` so Alpine hidden elements don't flash on load |
| [`base.html`](src/hiive_monitor/web/templates/base.html) | "Hiive" wordmark in brand green, 48px sticky nav, Hiive token classes throughout, widened `max-w-7xl` |
| [`brief.html`](src/hiive_monitor/web/templates/brief.html) | Two-column layout (flex-1 list + 288px sticky sidebar), dense 12-col grid table, rectangular badges (4px radius), 0.5px ghost borders, Pipeline/Severity/Simulation sidebar panels, all HTMX & Alpine behaviour preserved |
| [`main.py`](src/hiive_monitor/web/routes/main.py) | All inline HTMX response HTML updated to Hiive tokens (approve→`primary-fixed`, dismiss→`on-surface-variant`, error→`error-container`, tick polling→`surface-container-low`) |
| `output.css` | Rebuilt with new token set |

**Design docs updated:**

content.text:
Base directory for this skill: C:\Users\koush\.claude\plugins\cache\impeccable\impeccable\2.1.1\.claude\skills\impeccable

<post-update-cleanup>
BEFORE doing any design work, run this one-time maintenance step. Tell the user:

> **Impeccable was updated.** A few skills were renamed or merged in this version (e.g. `/arrange` is now `/layout`, `/normalize` was folded into `/polish`). I'll clean up the old skill files so they don't clutter your project. This only runs once after an update.

Then run:

```bash
node .claude/skills/impeccable/scripts/cleanup-deprecated.mjs
```

If the script removed files, briefly confirm what was cleaned up. If it found nothing, skip any output and move on.

After running the script, delete this entire section (from `<post-update-cleanup>` through `</post-update-cleanup>` inclusive) from THIS file so it does not run again until the next update. Save the file.
</post-update-cleanup>

This skill guides creation of distinctive, production-grade frontend interfaces that avoid generic "AI slop" aesthetics. Implement real working code with exceptional attention to aesthetic details and creative choices.

## Context Gathering Protocol

Design skills produce generic output without project context. You MUST have confirmed design context before doing any design work.

**Required context** (every design skill needs at minimum):
- **Target audience**: Who uses this product and in what context?
- **Use cases**: What jobs are they trying to get done?
- **Brand personality/tone**: How should the interface feel?

Individual skills may require additional context. Check the skill's preparation section for specifics.

**CRITICAL**: You cannot infer this context by reading the codebase. Code tells you what was built, not who it's for or what it should feel like. Only the creator can provide this context.

**Gathering order:**
1. **Check current instructions (instant)**: If your loaded instructions already contain a **Design Context** section, proceed immediately.
2. **Check .impeccable.md (fast)**: If not in instructions, read `.impeccable.md` from the project root. If it exists and contains the required context, proceed.
3. **Run impeccable teach (REQUIRED)**: If neither source has context, you MUST run /impeccable teach NOW before doing anything else. Do NOT skip this step. Do NOT attempt to infer context from the codebase instead.

---

## Design Direction

Commit to a BOLD aesthetic direction:
- **Purpose**: What problem does this interface solve? Who uses it?
- **Tone**: Pick an extreme: brutally minimal, maximalist chaos, retro-futuristic, organic/natural, luxury/refined, playful/toy-like, editorial/magazine, brutalist/raw, art deco/geometric, soft/pastel, industrial/utilitarian, etc. There are so many flavors to choose from. Use these for inspiration but design one that is true to the aesthetic direction.
- **Constraints**: Technical requirements (framework, performance, accessibility).
- **Differentiation**: What makes this UNFORGETTABLE? What's the one thing someone will remember?

**CRITICAL**: Choose a clear conceptual direction and execute it with precision. Bold maximalism and refined minimalism both work. The key is intentionality, not intensity.

Then implement working code that is:
- Production-grade and functional
- Visually striking and memorable
- Cohesive with a clear aesthetic point-of-view
- Meticulously refined in every detail

## Frontend Aesthetics Guidelines

### Typography
→ *Consult [typography reference](reference/typography.md) for OpenType features, web font loading, and the deeper material on scales.*

Choose fonts that are beautiful, unique, and interesting. Pair a distinctive display font with a refined body font.

<typography_principles>
Always apply these — do not consult a reference, just do them:

- Use a modular type scale with fluid sizing (clamp) for headings on marketing/content pages. Use fixed `rem` scales for app UIs and dashboards (no major design system uses fluid type in product UI).
- Use fewer sizes with more contrast. A 5-step scale with at least a 1.25 ratio between steps creates clearer hierarchy than 8 sizes that are 1.1× apart.
- Line-height scales inversely with line length. Narrow columns want tighter leading, wide columns want more. For light text on dark backgrounds, ADD 0.05-0.1 to your normal line-height — light type reads as lighter weight and needs more breathing room.
- Cap line length at ~65-75ch. Body text wider than that is fatiguing.
</typography_principles>

<font_selection_procedure>
DO THIS BEFORE TYPING ANY FONT NAME.

The model's natural failure mode is "I was told not to use Inter, so I will pick my next favorite font, which becomes the new monoculture." Avoid this by performing the following procedure on every project, in order:

Step 1. Read the brief once. Write down 3 concrete words for the brand voice (e.g., "warm and mechanical and opinionated", "calm and clinical and careful", "fast and dense and unimpressed", "handmade and a little weird"). NOT "modern" or "elegant" — those are dead categories.

Step 2. List the 3 fonts you would normally reach for given those words. Write them down. They are most likely from this list:

<reflex_fonts_to_reject>
Fraunces
Newsreader
Lora
Crimson
Crimson Pro
Crimson Text
Playfair Display
Cormorant
Cormorant Garamond
Syne
IBM Plex Mono
IBM Plex Sans
IBM Plex Serif
Space Mono
Space Grotesk
Inter
DM Sans
DM Serif Display
DM Serif Text
Outfit
Plus Jakarta Sans
Instrument Sans
Instrument Serif
</reflex_fonts_to_reject>

Reject every font that appears in the reflex_fonts_to_reject list. They are your training-data defaults and they create monoculture across projects.

Step 3. Browse a font catalog with the 3 brand words in mind. Sources: Google Fonts, Pangram Pangram, Future Fonts, Adobe Fonts, ABC Dinamo, Klim Type Foundry, Velvetyne. Look for something that fits the brand as a *physical object* — a museum exhibit caption, a hand-painted shop sign, a 1970s mainframe terminal manual, a fabric label on the inside of a coat, a children's book printed on cheap newsprint. Reject the first thing that "looks designy" — that's the trained reflex too. Keep looking.

Step 4. Cross-check the result. The right font for an "elegant" brief is NOT necessarily a serif. The right font for a "technical" brief is NOT necessarily a sans-serif. The right font for a "warm" brief is NOT Fraunces. If your final pick lines up with your reflex pattern, go back to Step 3.
</font_selection_procedure>

<typography_rules>
DO use a modular type scale with fluid sizing (clamp) on headings.
DO vary font weights and sizes to create clear visual hierarchy.
DO vary your font choices across projects. If you used a serif display font on the last project, look for a sans, monospace, or display face on this one.

DO NOT use overused fonts like Inter, Roboto, Arial, Open Sans, or system defaults — but also do not simply switch to your second-favorite. Every font in the reflex_fonts_to_reject list above is banned. Look further.
DO NOT use monospace typography as lazy shorthand for "technical/developer" vibes.
DO NOT put large icons with rounded corners above every heading. They rarely add value and make sites look templated.
DO NOT use only one font family for the entire page. Pair a distinctive display font with a refined body font.
DO NOT use a flat type hierarchy where sizes are too close together. Aim for at least a 1.25 ratio between steps.
DO NOT set long body passages in uppercase. Reserve all-caps for short labels and headings.
</typography_rules>

### Color & Theme
→ *Consult [color reference](reference/color-and-contrast.md) for the deeper material on contrast, accessibility, and palette construction.*

Commit to a cohesive palette. Dominant colors with sharp accents outperform timid, evenly-distributed palettes.

<color_principles>
Always apply these — do not consult a reference, just do them:

- Use OKLCH, not HSL. OKLCH is perceptually uniform: equal steps in lightness *look* equal, which HSL does not deliver. As you move toward white or black, REDUCE chroma — high chroma at extreme lightness looks garish. A light blue at 85% lightness wants ~0.08 chroma, not the 0.15 of your base color.
- Tint your neutrals toward your brand hue. Even a chroma of 0.005-0.01 is perceptible and creates subconscious cohesion between brand color and UI surfaces. The hue you tint toward should come from THIS brand, not from a "warm = friendly" or "cool = tech" formula. Pick the brand's actual hue first, then tint everything toward it.
- The 60-30-10 rule is about visual *weight*, not pixel count. 60% neutral / surface, 30% secondary text and borders, 10% accent. Accents work BECAUSE they're rare. Overuse kills their power.
</color_principles>

<theme_selection>
Theme (light vs dark) should be DERIVED from audience and viewing context, not picked from a default. Read the brief and ask: when is this product used, by whom, in what physical setting?

- A perp DEX consumed during fast trading sessions → dark
- A hospital portal consumed by anxious patients on phones late at night → light
- A children's reading app → light
- A vintage motorcycle forum where users sit in their garage at 9pm → dark
- An observability dashboard for SREs in a dark office → dark
- A wedding planning checklist for couples on a Sunday morning → light
- A music player app for headphone listening at night → dark
- A food magazine homepage browsed during a coffee break → light

Do not default everything to light "to play it safe." Do not default everything to dark "to look cool." Both defaults are the lazy reflex. The correct theme is the one the actual user wants in their actual context.
</theme_selection>

<color_rules>
DO use modern CSS color functions (oklch, color-mix, light-dark) for perceptually uniform, maintainable palettes.
DO tint your neutrals toward your brand hue. Even a subtle hint creates subconscious cohesion.

DO NOT use gray text on colored backgrounds; it looks washed out. Use a shade of the background color instead.
DO NOT use pure black (#000) or pure white (#fff). Always tint; pure black/white never appears in nature.
DO NOT use the AI color palette: cyan-on-dark, purple-to-blue gradients, neon accents on dark backgrounds.
DO NOT use gradient text for impact — see <absolute_bans> below for the strict definition. Solid colors only for text.
DO NOT default to dark mode with glowing accents. It looks "cool" without requiring actual design decisions.
DO NOT default to light mode "to be safe" either. The point is to choose, not to retreat to a safe option.
</color_rules>

### Layout & Space
→ *Consult [spatial reference](reference/spatial-design.md) for the deeper material on grids, container queries, and optical adjustments.*

Create visual rhythm through varied spacing, not the same padding everywhere. Embrace asymmetry and unexpected compositions. Break the grid intentionally for emphasis.

<spatial_principles>
Always apply these — do not consult a reference, just do them:

- Use a 4pt spacing scale with semantic token names (`--space-sm`, `--space-md`), not pixel-named (`--spacing-8`). Scale: 4, 8, 12, 16, 24, 32, 48, 64, 96. 8pt is too coarse — you'll often want 12px between two values.
- Use `gap` instead of margins for sibling spacing. It eliminates margin collapse and the cleanup hacks that come with it.
- Vary spacing for hierarchy. A heading with extra space above it reads as more important — make use of that. Don't apply the same padding everywhere.
- Self-adjusting grid pattern: `grid-template-columns: repeat(auto-fit, minmax(280px, 1fr))` is the breakpoint-free responsive grid for card-style content.
- Container queries are for components, viewport queries are for page layout. A card in a sidebar should adapt to the sidebar's width, not the viewport's.
</spatial_principles>

<spatial_rules>
DO create visual rhythm through varied spacing: tight groupings, generous separations.
DO use fluid spacing with clamp() that breathes on larger screens.
DO use asymmetry and unexpected compositions; break the grid intentionally for emphasis.

DO NOT wrap everything in cards. Not everything needs a container.
DO NOT nest cards inside cards. Visual noise; flatten the hierarchy.
DO NOT use identical card grids (same-sized cards with icon + heading + text, repeated endlessly).
DO NOT use the hero metric layout template (big number, small label, supporting stats, gradient accent).
DO NOT center everything. Left-aligned text with asymmetric layouts feels more designed.
DO NOT use the same spacing everywhere. Without rhythm, layouts feel monotonous.
DO NOT let body text wrap beyond ~80 characters per line. Add a max-width like 65–75ch so the eye can track easily.
</spatial_rules>

### Visual Details

<absolute_bans>
These CSS patterns are NEVER acceptable. They are the most recognizable AI design tells. Match-and-refuse: if you find yourself about to write any of these, stop and rewrite the element with a different structure entirely.

BAN 1: Side-stripe borders on cards/list items/callouts/alerts
  - PATTERN: `border-left:` or `border-right:` with width greater than 1px
  - INCLUDES: hard-coded colors AND CSS variables
  - FORBIDDEN: `border-left: 3px solid red`, `border-left: 4px solid #ff0000`, `border-left: 4px solid var(--color-warning)`, `border-left: 5px solid oklch(...)`, etc.
  - WHY: this is the single most overused "design touch" in admin, dashboard, and medical UIs. It never looks intentional regardless of color, radius, opacity, or whether the variable name is "primary" or "warning" or "accent."
  - REWRITE: use a different element structure entirely. Do not just swap to box-shadow inset. Reach for full borders, background tints, leading numbers/icons, or no visual indicator at all.

BAN 2: Gradient text
  - PATTERN: `background-clip: text` (or `-webkit-background-clip: text`) combined with a gradient background
  - FORBIDDEN: any combination that makes text fill come from a `linear-gradient`, `radial-gradient`, or `conic-gradient`
  - WHY: gradient text is decorative rather than meaningful and is one of the top three AI design tells
  - REWRITE: use a single solid color for text. If you want emphasis, use weight or size, not gradient fill.
</absolute_bans>

DO: Use intentional, purposeful decorative elements that reinforce brand.
DO NOT: Use border-left or border-right greater than 1px as a colored accent stripe on cards, list items, callouts, or alerts. See <absolute_bans> above for the strict CSS pattern.
DO NOT: Use glassmorphism everywhere (blur effects, glass cards, glow borders used decoratively rather than purposefully).
DO NOT: Use sparklines as decoration. Tiny charts that look sophisticated but convey nothing meaningful.
DO NOT: Use rounded rectangles with generic drop shadows. Safe, forgettable, could be any AI output.
DO NOT: Use modals unless there's truly no better alternative. Modals are lazy.

### Motion
→ *Consult [motion reference](reference/motion-design.md) for timing, easing, and reduced motion.*

Focus on high-impact moments: one well-orchestrated page load with staggered reveals creates more delight than scattered micro-interactions.

**DO**: Use motion to convey state changes: entrances, exits, feedback
**DO**: Use exponential easing (ease-out-quart/quint/expo) for natural deceleration
**DO**: For height animations, use grid-template-rows transitions instead of animating height directly
**DON'T**: Animate layout properties (width, height, padding, margin). Use transform and opacity only
**DON'T**: Use bounce or elastic easing. They feel dated and tacky; real objects decelerate smoothly

### Interaction
→ *Consult [interaction reference](reference/interaction-design.md) for forms, focus, and loading patterns.*

Make interactions feel fast. Use optimistic UI: update immediately, sync later.

**DO**: Use progressive disclosure. Start simple, reveal sophistication through interaction (basic options first, advanced behind expandable sections; hover states that reveal secondary actions)
**DO**: Design empty states that teach the interface, not just say "nothing here"
**DO**: Make every interactive surface feel intentional and responsive
**DON'T**: Repeat the same information (redundant headers, intros that restate the heading)
**DON'T**: Make every button primary. Use ghost buttons, text links, secondary styles; hierarchy matters

### Responsive
→ *Consult [responsive reference](reference/responsive-design.md) for mobile-first, fluid design, and container queries.*

**DO**: Use container queries (@container) for component-level responsiveness
**DO**: Adapt the interface for different contexts, not just shrink it
**DON'T**: Hide critical functionality on mobile. Adapt the interface, don't amputate it

### UX Writing
→ *Consult [ux-writing reference](reference/ux-writing.md) for labels, errors, and empty states.*

**DO**: Make every word earn its place
**DON'T**: Repeat information users can already see

---

## The AI Slop Test

**Critical quality check**: If you showed this interface to someone and said "AI made this," would they believe you immediately? If yes, that's the problem.

A distinctive interface should make someone ask "how was this made?" not "which AI made this?"

Review the DON'T guidelines above. They are the fingerprints of AI-generated work from 2024-2025.

---

## Implementation Principles

Match implementation complexity to the aesthetic vision. Maximalist designs need elaborate code with extensive animations and effects. Minimalist or refined designs need restraint, precision, and careful attention to spacing, typography, and subtle details.

Interpret creatively and make unexpected choices that feel genuinely designed for the context. No design should be the same. Vary between light and dark themes, different fonts, different aesthetics. NEVER converge on common choices across generations.

Remember: Claude is capable of extraordinary creative work. Don't hold back. Show what can truly be created when thinking outside the box and committing fully to a distinctive vision.

---

## Craft Mode

If this skill is invoked with the argument "craft" (e.g., `/impeccable craft [feature description]`), follow the [craft flow](reference/craft.md). Pass any additional arguments as the feature description.

---

## Teach Mode

If this skill is invoked with the argument "teach" (e.g., `/impeccable teach`), skip all design work above and instead run the teach flow below. This is a one-time setup that gathers design context for the project.

### Step 1: Explore the Codebase

Before asking questions, thoroughly scan the project to discover what you can:

- **README and docs**: Project purpose, target audience, any stated goals
- **Package.json / config files**: Tech stack, dependencies, existing design libraries
- **Existing components**: Current design patterns, spacing, typography in use
- **Brand assets**: Logos, favicons, color values already defined
- **Design tokens / CSS variables**: Existing color palettes, font stacks, spacing scales
- **Any style guides or brand documentation**

Note what you've learned and what remains unclear.

### Step 2: Ask UX-Focused Questions

STOP and call the AskUserQuestion tool to clarify. Focus only on what you couldn't infer from the codebase:

#### Users & Purpose
- Who uses this? What's their context when using it?
- What job are they trying to get done?
- What emotions should the interface evoke? (confidence, delight, calm, urgency, etc.)

#### Brand & Personality
- How would you describe the brand personality in 3 words?
- Any reference sites or apps that capture the right feel? What specifically about them?
- What should this explicitly NOT look like? Any anti-references?

#### Aesthetic Preferences
- Any strong preferences for visual direction? (minimal, bold, elegant, playful, technical, organic, etc.)
- Light mode, dark mode, or both?
- Any colors that must be used or avoided?

#### Accessibility & Inclusion
- Specific accessibility requirements? (WCAG level, known user needs)
- Considerations for reduced motion, color blindness, or other accommodations?

Skip questions where the answer is already clear from the codebase exploration.

### Step 3: Write Design Context

Synthesize your findings and the user's answers into a `## Design Context` section:

```markdown
## Design Context

### Users
[Who they are, their context, the job to be done]

### Brand Personality
[Voice, tone, 3-word personality, emotional goals]

### Aesthetic Direction
[Visual tone, references, anti-references, theme]

### Design Principles
[3-5 principles derived from the conversation that should guide all design decisions]
```

Write this section to `.impeccable.md` in the project root. If the file already exists, update the Design Context section in place.

Then STOP and call the AskUserQuestion tool to clarify. whether they'd also like the Design Context appended to CLAUDE.md. If yes, append or update the section there as well.

Confirm completion and summarize the key design principles that will now guide all future work.

---

## Extract Mode

If this skill is invoked with the argument "extract" (e.g., `/impeccable extract [target]`), follow the [extract flow](reference/extract.md). Pass any additional arguments as the extraction target.

ARGUMENTS: 


The following is a sample UI design for the per deal details page. please use it only as a reference and redesign our existing page based on the current functionalities we have in the page. if there are some UI components/features in the sample html that we think would be useful later when we expand the products, then extract all the necessary parts from it and update .impeccable.md an DESIGN.md


## SAMPLE HTML CODE

<!DOCTYPE html>

lastPrompt: /impeccable:impeccable The following is a sample UI design for the per deal details page. please use it only as a reference and redesign our existing page based on the current functionalities we have…

content.thinking:
Now I have a clear picture of the existing `deal_detail.html`. Let me analyze:

**Current functionality:**
1. **Header**: Deal ID + stage badge + issuer name/shares/price + "← Brief" link + debug link
2. **Deal facts**: 4-column grid of key-value facts (Stage entered, Responsible, ROFR deadline, Buyer)
3. **Event timeline**: Reverse-sorted list of events with date, event_type badge, summary
4. **Agent observations**: Expandable observation cards with severity badge, dimensions triggered, reasoning (show/hide)
   - Expandable shows: severity_rationale, enrichment_chain steps, optional debug JSON


5. **Interventions**: List of interventions with timestamp, type, status, subject/body preview

I have access to all the data I need: the deal record with its key fields, issuer information, events, enriched observations with parsed reasoning, and interventions with their metadata.

Now I'm sketching out the layout structure—a breadcrumb at the top, followed by a header showing the deal ID, issuer name, stage and severity badges, then a 4-column grid with deal facts on the left, agent reasoning and the latest drafted intervention in the center, and the timeline on the right. I'll adapt the sample design to fit these three main sections, making sure the agent reasoning and intervention draft are the focal point.

Looking at what needs to change from the current page—right now it shows all observations and interventions, but the sample only highlights the most recent one, so I should compress the display to show just the latest agent observation and pending intervention rather than the full history. I'm realizing the deal detail page should stay read-only for context and history, while the brief page remains where analysts actually take action on interventions. I'll keep the layout simple: deal facts on the left, the most recent observation and any pending interventions in the center, and a compact timeline with historical data on the right. I need to pull the latest observation's severity for the page header badge using a Jinja filter to sort by timestamp and grab the first result. For the severity rationale styling, I'm avoiding the left border stripe from the sample and instead applying a subtle background tint with a matching border—using error-container for ESCALATE, primary-fixed for ACT, and similar treatment for WATCH.

For the timeline, I'm going with a vertical layout showing dots connected by lines, where each dot's color indicates the event type: primary-container for outbound communications, secondary for inbound, outline-variant for stage transitions, and surface-container-high as the default.

The dimensions section will display the triggered dimension names as pills since I don't have severity data to work with. For active interventions, I need to decide whether to include approve/dismiss actions on the deal detail page—the routes are already set up to use HTMX for swapping the intervention cards in place.

Looking at the current implementation, the deal detail page keeps interventions read-only, which makes sense since the brief page is the primary action surface. I'll stick with that approach to avoid expanding scope. Now I'm mapping out the final layout structure for the deal detail page. I'm restructuring the layout to better balance the information—moving interventions to the left sidebar with deal facts, keeping the agent analysis front and center with full observations, and putting the timeline on the right. Now I'm implementing the event type styling using the design tokens, mapping communication events to green variants, stage transitions to neutral surfaces, and applying the appropriate text colors for contrast.

Writing the timeline component... Writing the template structure... Writing the header markup... Writing the template markup... Building out the layout structure... Building the observation card layout... Writing the enrichment chain display... Building the template layout... Building the expandable observation details...

I'm noticing there's a Jinja2 templating issue with the slice syntax I used — I need to handle that differently, maybe with a filter or by setting up the variable properly in the backend before passing it to the template. I'm working through how to apply the Hiive token palette to the divider styling. Since Tailwind's `divide-*` utilities might not support custom colors, I'll add `border-t-[0.5px] border-outline-variant/50` directly to each observation row to create the separators between items.

Now I need to handle the Jinja2 sorting—converting the filtered observations to a list so I can slice them properly, then write out the template code.

Looking at the data structures I'm working with: observations have `observed_at`, `severity`, `reasoning`, and a parsed reasoning dict with rationale and triggered dimensions; interventions have `created_at`, `intervention_type`, `status`, and draft content; events have `occurred_at` and `event_type`. I'll use these attributes to structure the timeline display.

Now I'm setting up the three-column layout with a 4-column grid where the left and right sidebars each take one column and the center content spans two columns. For the event timeline, I'm planning a vertical layout that chains these records together chronologically.

Building the timeline component... I'm working through the spacing calculations for the timeline dots and text alignment. The dot is 12 pixels wide positioned at the left edge, so the vertical line should run through its center at 6 pixels. With 24 pixels of left padding on the text row, there's a 12-pixel gap between where the dot ends and the text begins, which gives good visual separation.

For the implementation, I'm considering a simpler approach using a left border on the container with appropriate margins and padding to position the dots relative to that line.

Building the timeline markup... I'm realizing the pixel-based positioning is getting messy, so I'm switching to a cleaner flexbox layout instead where the dot sits in a fixed-width container and the event details flow naturally beside it.

But looking at the sample's approach more carefully, I see how the vertical line works—it's created with a left border on the container, and the dots are positioned absolutely relative to that line using negative left margins. Let me adopt that pattern for a more polished timeline effect. I'm simplifying this approach—using a cleaner structure with a left border and flexbox layout instead of trying to calculate precise dot positioning with negative margins and offsets.

Building the timeline markup... Writing the timeline component... I've settled on using the `›` character as a breadcrumb separator since we don't have Material Icons available. Now I'm writing out the complete template for the deal detail page, starting with the breadcrumb navigation that links back to the Daily Brief and displays the current deal ID and issuer name. Building the status badges... Building the layout structure... Writing the template markup... Writing the intervention details... Building the agent analysis section... Writing the dimensions section... Building the enrichment chain display... Building the collapsible section for older observations... Writing the severity badge markup... Building the template layout... Writing the event timeline template...

I'm noticing I used `divide-y` for the interventions section, but I should double-check how that utility works compared to `space-y` to make sure I'm applying the right spacing approach here. The `first:` modifier in Tailwind applies the border removal only to the first child, so the specificity should work correctly—the `first:border-t-0` rule will override the `border-t-[0.5px]` for that element.

Actually, I think the simpler approach is better: conditionally apply the border classes only to non-first items using a template check, rather than relying on CSS specificity. Now I need to look at the `format_number` filter implementation. Writing the complete template file...

I'm identifying which design patterns from the sample to reject—specifically the side stripe borders on active nav items and severity indicators—and replacing them with background tint blocks instead for a cleaner, more consistent look.
